cheatSheet:
# Design Patterns Quick Reference

## Creational
| Pattern | Purpose | When to Use |
|---------|---------|------------|
| Singleton | One instance | DB connection, config |
| Factory | Create without specifying class | Runtime type selection |
| Builder | Complex construction | Many optional params |

## Structural
| Pattern | Purpose | When to Use |
|---------|---------|------------|
| Adapter | Interface conversion | Legacy integration |
| Decorator | Add behavior dynamically | Logging, caching |
| Facade | Simple interface | Complex subsystems |

## Behavioral
| Pattern | Purpose | When to Use |
|---------|---------|------------|
| Observer | Event notification | UI updates, pub/sub |
| Strategy | Swappable algorithms | Payment, sorting |
| Command | Encapsulate actions | Undo/redo, queuing |

sessions.title: Anti-Patterns and Code Smells
sessions.content:
## Anti-Patterns: What NOT to Do

Knowing anti-patterns is as important as knowing patterns — they show up in code reviews and interviews.

### God Object
One class that does everything. Violates SRP.
```python
# BAD: 2000-line UserManager class
class UserManager:
    def create_user(self): ...
    def send_email(self): ...
    def generate_invoice(self): ...
    def upload_avatar(self): ...
    def calculate_shipping(self): ...  # Why is this here?!
```
**Fix**: Split into focused classes (UserService, EmailService, etc.)

### Premature Optimization
"We might need to cache this" — before you even have 10 users.
**Rule**: Make it work, make it right, make it fast (in that order).

### Golden Hammer
Using the same tool for everything. "I know Redis, so every problem is a caching problem."
**Fix**: Choose the right tool for each problem.

### Cargo Cult Programming
Copying patterns without understanding why. Using microservices for a 2-person startup.
**Fix**: Understand the WHY before applying any pattern.

### Spaghetti Code
No structure, everything depends on everything. Can't change one thing without breaking another.
**Fix**: Apply SOLID principles, establish clear module boundaries.

### Common Code Smells
| Smell | Sign | Fix |
|-------|------|-----|
| Long method | > 20 lines | Extract methods |
| Long parameter list | > 3 params | Use objects/builder |
| Duplicate code | Same logic in 2+ places | Extract to shared function |
| Feature envy | Class uses another class's data more than its own | Move method to the other class |
| Shotgun surgery | One change requires editing 10 files | Better encapsulation |

### Interview Tip
"In code reviews, I look for SRP violations first — they're the most common smell. A class with 'and' in its description ('this class handles authentication AND logging AND error reporting') is a red flag."

activities.description: Code review exercise: find anti-patterns in sample code

reviewQuestions: What's the difference between a design pattern and an anti-pattern?
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify anti-patterns in any codebase

quizBank.question: Which SOLID principle does the Factory pattern primarily support?
quizBank.explanation: Factory lets you add new types without modifying existing code (Open for extension, Closed for modification).

quizBank.question: What is the Decorator pattern?
quizBank.explanation: Decorator wraps an object to add behavior dynamically, without modifying the original class. Like adding toppings to ice cream.

quizBank.question: When should you NOT use Singleton?
quizBank.explanation: Avoid Singleton when: you need testability (global state is hard to mock), when multiple instances might be needed later, or when dependency injection is available.

quizBank.question: Identify the anti-pattern in this code: class ReportManager handles user authentication, data fetching, PDF generation, and email sending.
quizBank.explanation: God Object anti-pattern. The class has too many responsibilities. Split into AuthService, DataService, ReportGenerator, and EmailService.

interviewTips: Know SOLID cold — it comes up in every senior interview
Don't over-engineer: use the simplest pattern that solves the problem
Be ready to draw UML-style diagrams for any pattern
Know anti-patterns as well as patterns — shows maturity
